lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: This castle hath a pleasant seat; the air

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Nimbly and sweetly recommends itself

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Unto our gentle senses.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: This guest of summer,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: The temple-haunting martlet, does approve,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: By his loved mansionry, that the heaven’s breath

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Smells wooingly here: no jutty, frieze,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Buttress, nor coign of vantage, but this bird

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Hath made his pendent bed and procreant cradle:

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Where they most breed and haunt, I have observed,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: The air is delicate.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: See, see, our honour’d hostess!

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: The love that follows us sometime is our trouble,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Which still we thank as love. Herein I teach you

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: How you shall bid God ’ild us for your pains,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And thank us for your trouble.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: All our service

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: In every point twice done and then done double

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Were poor and single business to contend

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Against those honours deep and broad wherewith

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Your majesty loads our house: for those of old,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: And the late dignities heap’d up to them,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: We rest your hermits.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Where’s the thane of Cawdor?

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: We coursed him at the heels, and had a purpose

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: To be his purveyor: but he rides well;

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And his great love, sharp as his spur, hath holp him

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: To his home before us. Fair and noble hostess,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: We are your guest to-night.

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Your servants ever

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Have theirs, themselves and what is theirs, in compt,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: To make their audit at your highness’ pleasure,

lines.speaker: LADY MACBETH
lines.text: Still to return your own.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Give me your hand;

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Conduct me to mine host: we love him highly,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And shall continue our graces towards him.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: By your leave, hostess.

stage_directions.text: Hautboys and torches. Enter Duncan, Malcolm, Donalbain, Banquo, Lennox, Macduff, Ross, Angus, and Attendants.

glosses.definition: house martin (swallow) — a bird said to nest only in holy places
a corner providing a vantage point
hanging nest and nursery — where the martlet breeds
praying men — religious figures who pray for their benefactors
an officer sent ahead to prepare for a royal arrival

annotations.title: Duncan's last approval
annotations.body:
Duncan and Banquo praise the sweet air of Macbeth's castle while Lady Macbeth waits inside to kill him. Banquo notes the martlet — a bird said to nest only in holy places — has made its home on the walls. The dramatic irony is unbearable: every word of their praise is a misreading of the place they are about to enter.

The scene is Shakespeare at his most cruel. He does not allow Duncan a moment of suspicion. The king is graceful, charming, trusting. His thanks to Lady Macbeth ('this honoured hostess') is delivered as courtesy, and she answers in perfect protocol. The audience has thirty-seven lines to watch the trap be set politely.

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare have Banquo note the martlet nests on the castle walls?
options: To set up dramatic irony — martlets nest only in holy places, but this house is about to become a site of regicide
Because Banquo is an amateur naturalist

trials.prompt: Duncan calls Lady Macbeth 'this honoured hostess.' The dramatic irony is:
options: She is preparing to help murder him
She is about to divorce her husband
